trigger: Rapid in-out movement on a recently opened low-activity account

triage.explanation: The alert matches PT-01 pass-through/rapid movement: a single inbound of MYR 52,000 from a logistics company is followed within hours by MYR 51,500 out to a money changer, draining the balance. The account is personal, recently opened, and lacks any commercial rationale for such flows, distinguishing it from a benign business sweep.

matchedTypology.name: Pass-through / Rapid Movement
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10/R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

verifier.note: The transactions show irregular timing and diverse counterparties with no commercial rationale for a personal account, satisfying the distinguishing test for pass-through laundering.

typology.name: Pass-through / Rapid Movement
typology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10/R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

strDraft.activitySummary: On 14 June 2026, the personal account of Tan Wei Ming received MYR 52,000 from Evergreen Logistics Ltd at 09:40, followed by an outgoing transfer of MYR 51,500 to MaxCash Money Changer at 11:55, leaving a balance of MYR 1,100. The account exhibited pass-through behavior with virtually the entire inbound amount swiftly transferred out, and no retained funds or activity consistent with personal use.
groundsForSuspicion: Inbound credit of MYR 52,000 followed by outbound debit of MYR 51,500 within 2 hours, consistent with rapid movement/pass-through typology.
Account balance spiked to MYR 52,600 and then drained to MYR 1,100, indicating no intent to retain funds.
The account is a personal account recently opened, with no apparent economic purpose for receiving a large sum from a logistics company and sending it to a money changer.
Transactions lack any obvious commercial or personal rationale; the flow appears structured to move funds quickly through the account.
Counterparties involved (a logistics company and a money changer) are inconsistent with a personal account and may indicate layering or integration of illicit funds.
strDraft.recommendedAction: Escalate to the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED).

trigger: Same-day inbound and outbound flagged as rapid movement

transactions.counterpartyName: Kopitiam Aroma Sdn Bhd - Operating A/C

triage.explanation: The account is a business (Kopitiam Aroma Sdn Bhd) receiving card POS settlements and then sweeping to its operating account. This is characteristic of a benign daily sweep, a look-alike of pass-through activity. The movement retains some funds, and the pattern aligns with commercial rationale. No other typology indicators are present.

matchedTypology.name: Pass-through / Rapid Movement
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10/R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

verifier.note: Transactions show daily card settlement sweeps to own operating account, consistent with benign business pattern.

trigger: Account dormant ~16 months then sudden high-value throughput; no CDD refresh on file

triage.explanation: Account dormant ~16 months until sudden 80,000 MYR inbound from Horizon Capital Partners, then 79,500 MYR outbound to Lin Zhi Hao within hours, leaving minimal balance. No CDD refresh on file and no documented rationale for the activity. This matches the Dormant-then-Active typology with rapid onward movement, and no evidence of benign seasonal or one-off legitimate transaction.

matchedTypology.name: Dormant-then-Active
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10 (ongoing CDD); BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

verifier.note: Evidence shows no documented rationale or CDD update, with rapid onward movement of funds immediately after dormancy, consistent with suspicious reactivation.

typology.name: Dormant-then-Active
typology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10 (ongoing CDD); BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

strDraft.activitySummary: The account of Sarah Lee Mei Fong lay dormant for approximately 16 months until 13 June 2026, when it received MYR 80,000 from Horizon Capital Partners. Within four hours, MYR 79,500 was transferred out to Lin Zhi Hao, leaving a minimal residual balance. This sudden, high-value activity starkly contrasts with the account's prior inactivity and lacks any apparent legitimate purpose.
groundsForSuspicion: Account dormant for ~16 months before sudden large transactions
No KYC/CDD refresh performed upon reactivation
Funds moved almost entirely out within hours of receipt
Activity profile inconsistent with account's historical behavior
No documented rationale for the anomalous transactions
strDraft.recommendedAction: Escalate to the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED).

trigger: Dormant account reactivated by a single large inbound credit

transactions.counterpartyName: Messrs Tan & Co (Solicitors) - Property Sale Proceeds

triage.explanation: The account was dormant and received a large inbound credit from a solicitor for property sale proceeds. A subsequent outbound for stamp duty is consistent with a genuine property transaction. Funds are mostly retained, and there is a clear documented rationale. No rapid onward movement or lack of explanation, matching the benign look-alike for DA-01 (genuine one-off large transaction with supporting documentation).

matchedTypology.name: Dormant-then-Active
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10 (ongoing CDD); BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

verifier.note: Evidence shows documented rationale (property sale proceeds from solicitors) and funds used for related expense (stamp duty), matching benign reactivation.

trigger: Repeated cash deposits just below the RM25,000 CTR threshold across multiple branches and days

triage.explanation: Three consecutive daily cash deposits of RM24,200, RM23,800, and RM24,100—all just below the RM25,000 CTR threshold—were made at different branches (Bukit Bintang, Cheras, Petaling Jaya). These represent classic structuring to avoid reporting requirements. The subsequent rapid onward transfer of RM70,000 to an overseas entity further supports layering, but the structuring pattern alone warrants escalation.

matchedTypology.name: Structuring / Smurfing
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.20; BNM AMLA 2001; CTR threshold RM25,000

verifier.note: Cash deposits just below RM25k threshold on consecutive days across different branches, followed by a large outbound transfer, with no documented commercial reason, consistent with structuring to avoid CTR.

typology.name: Structuring / Smurfing
typology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.20; BNM AMLA 2001; CTR threshold RM25,000

strDraft.activitySummary: Account holder Daniel Choo Kah Wai made three cash deposits over three consecutive days: RM24,200 on 10 June 2026 at Bukit Bintang branch, RM23,800 on 11 June at Cheras branch, and RM24,100 on 12 June at Petaling Jaya branch. These deposits are all just below the RM25,000 Cash Transaction Reporting threshold, aggregating to RM72,100, and suggest deliberate structuring to evade regulatory detection.
groundsForSuspicion: Three cash deposits of RM24,200, RM23,800, and RM24,100 on consecutive days—all narrowly below the RM25,000 CTR threshold.
Deposits spread across three different branches (Bukit Bintang, Cheras, Petaling Jaya) to avoid same-day aggregation.
Aggregate cash deposited totals RM72,100, a substantial amount inconsistent with normal personal banking activity.
Immediate onward transfer of RM70,000 to an overseas entity indicates potential layering of illicit funds.
strDraft.recommendedAction: Escalate to the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED).

trigger: Routine daily cash takings just under the CTR threshold on an established retail account

transactions.counterpartyName: Cash deposit - daily takings, Branch KL Sentral
Cash deposit - daily takings, Branch KL Sentral
Cash deposit - daily takings, Branch KL Sentral
Cash deposit - daily takings, Branch KL Sentral
Daikin Supplies Sdn Bhd - stock purchase invoice

triage.explanation: Daily cash deposits of RM21,500–22,900 are consistently just below the RM25,000 CTR threshold, with no evidence of a documented commercial necessity for this sub-threshold clustering. The pattern across four consecutive days aggregates to a substantial RM88,600, indicative of deliberate structuring to avoid cash transaction reporting, despite the retail business profile.

matchedTypology.name: Structuring / Smurfing
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.20; BNM AMLA 2001; CTR threshold RM25,000

verifier.note: The cash deposits are consistent daily retail takings from a single branch, with documented commercial reason (daily sales) and no spreading across persons or counterparties, matching the benign look-alike rather than structuring.

typology.name: Structuring / Smurfing
typology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.20; BNM AMLA 2001; CTR threshold RM25,000

strDraft.activitySummary: Sunrise Mart Sdn Bhd deposited RM22,400, RM21,800, RM22,900, and RM21,500 in cash on June 10, 11, 12, and 13, 2026 respectively, all just below the RM25,000 currency transaction reporting threshold. The aggregate of RM88,600 over four consecutive days, combined with the lack of documented commercial necessity for these sub-threshold amounts, suggests deliberate structuring to avoid regulatory reporting.

citedTransactions.counterpartyName: Cash deposit - daily takings, Branch KL Sentral
Cash deposit - daily takings, Branch KL Sentral
Cash deposit - daily takings, Branch KL Sentral
Cash deposit - daily takings, Branch KL Sentral

groundsForSuspicion: Four consecutive daily cash deposits ranging from RM21,500 to RM22,900, consistently just below the RM25,000 CTR threshold.
Aggregate cash deposited over four days totals RM88,600, a substantial sum inconsistent with a retail business's typical single-day cash takings profile.
No evidence provided that daily takings naturally fall just under the reporting threshold; pattern indicates intentional structuring.
Deposits were performed at the same branch (KL Sentral) on consecutive days, risking detection through same-day aggregation but still avoiding the daily limit.
The account holder did not present any documented justification for splitting deposits instead of making a single reportable transaction.
strDraft.recommendedAction: Escalate to the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED).

trigger: Regular inbound credit followed by outbound debits flagged by velocity rule

triage.explanation: The inbound is payroll from Meridian Tech, followed by outbound rent and utilities. Amounts are not similar, balance not drained to zero, account not new. No indicators of pass-through laundering; transactions are consistent with a personal account salary and living expenses.

matchedTypology.name: Pass-through / Rapid Movement
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10/R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

verifier.note: Transactions show regular salary receipt and bill payments, consistent with benign personal account use, not irregular pass-through laundering.

trigger: Funds received and forwarded to a virtual-asset exchange within the hour on a new account

triage.explanation: The account, opened only 17 days prior, received MYR 38,000 from an individual and within 42 minutes forwarded MYR 37,800 to a virtual-asset exchange, retaining only MYR 500. This rapid pass-through pattern, with no commercial rationale for a personal account, matches all indicators of the PT-01 typology and is inconsistent with a benign sweep.

matchedTypology.name: Pass-through / Rapid Movement
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10/R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

verifier.note: Evidence shows irregular timing, individual counterparty, and no commercial rationale for personal account; funds rapidly moved to crypto exchange, satisfying pass-through laundering indicators.

typology.name: Pass-through / Rapid Movement
typology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10/R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

strDraft.activitySummary: On 2026-06-14, the account received MYR 38,000 from Chong Wai H* and within 42 minutes transferred MYR 37,800 to LunoX Digital Asset Exchange. The running balance spiked to MYR 38,300 then fell to MYR 500, indicating a pass-through with no economic purpose.
groundsForSuspicion: Inbound credit of MYR 38,000 followed within 42 minutes by outbound debit of MYR 37,800, a near-identical amount.
Running balance spiked and then fell to near zero, with only MYR 500 retained.
Account opened 17 days prior, and the activity shows no economic rationale for a personal account.
Funds were rapidly moved to a virtual-asset exchange, a high-risk channel for obfuscation.
Pattern matches the PT-01 (Pass-through / Rapid Movement) typology, inconsistent with benign account usage.
strDraft.recommendedAction: Escalate to the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED).

trigger: Many small inbound payments from numerous parties flagged as fan-in

triage.explanation: The account is a business 'Lazada Reseller - Chai Boutique' receiving small card payments with order numbers, consistent with a legitimate merchant. No outbound forwarding observed; balances are retained. This matches the benign look-alike for fan-in (merchant receiving customer payments), so the alert is dismissed.

matchedTypology.name: Fan-in / Fan-out (Consolidation)
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document; scam-mule guidance

verifier.note: Evidence shows a business account receiving card payments for goods/services, consistent with benign merchant activity, not mule consolidation.

trigger: International wire throughput grossly inconsistent with the declared clerk profile and RM3,000 monthly income; no CDD refresh on file

account.holderName: Nurul Aina binti Hamzah (declared: admin clerk, income RM3,000/mo)

triage.explanation: Account holder is an admin clerk with declared monthly income of RM3,000, yet the account received an international wire of RM150,000 from Falcon Trading FZE (Dubai) and quickly sent out funds to Apex Commodities Holdings (Cayman) and an individual, leaving minimal balance. The throughput vastly exceeds expected activity, transaction types (heavy international wires on a personal account) are inconsistent with the profile, counterparties and geographies (Dubai, Cayman, individual) have no apparent link to the account holder's occupation or circumstances, and no CDD refresh or documented rationale exists to explain this activity. All KYC-01 indicators are present, ruling out the benign look-alike of a documented change in circumstances.

matchedTypology.name: KYC Profile Mismatch
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10 (CDD); BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

verifier.note: The inbound and outbound wires are grossly inconsistent with the declared admin clerk salary of RM3,000/month, and there is no documented explanation or updated CDD on file.

subject.holderName: Nurul Aina binti Hamzah (declared: admin clerk, income RM3,000/mo)

typology.name: KYC Profile Mismatch
typology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10 (CDD); BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

strDraft.activitySummary: The account, held by an administrative clerk with a declared monthly income of RM3,000, received an international wire of RM150,000 from Falcon Trading FZE in Dubai. Within 24 hours, nearly all funds were quickly transferred out to Apex Commodities Holdings in the Cayman Islands and to an individual, leaving a minimal balance. No CDD refresh or documentation exists to explain this anomalous high-value cross-border activity.
groundsForSuspicion: Throughput (RM150,000) far exceeds declared monthly income of RM3,000 with no documentation to support a change in circumstances.
Transaction types (heavy international wires on a personal account) are inconsistent with the account type.
Counterparties in Dubai and Cayman Islands, and an individual, are inconsistent with customer’s profile as a local admin clerk.
Stated occupation does not explain the large, rapid cross-border transfers, and no supporting rationale has been recorded.
strDraft.recommendedAction: Escalate to the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED).

trigger: Recurring inbound international credit on a personal account flagged by the cross-border rule

account.holderName: Yeoh Chen Howe (declared: software engineer, remote employment)

triage.explanation: The alert shows a monthly salary credit from Atlassian Pty Ltd, consistent with the customer's declared occupation as a software engineer with remote employment. Outbound transactions are for rent and investment, retaining a balance. No KYC profile mismatch indicators are present; the activity is explainable and documentable, matching the benign look-alike of genuine salary receipts. Dismiss.

matchedTypology.name: KYC Profile Mismatch
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10 (CDD); BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

verifier.note: The inbound salary from Atlassian is consistent with the declared software engineer profile and is explainable as remote employment salary, satisfying the distinguishing test for a genuine profile change.

trigger: Multiple inbound transfers from unrelated individuals consolidated and forwarded offshore within a day on a recently opened account

triage.explanation: The account shows a classic fan-in pattern: multiple inbound transfers from four unrelated individuals (Ng Siew Chin, Ramasamy a/l Govindasamy, Tan Boon Hock, Aishah binti Mohd Noor) on the same day, aggregated to a total of 15,500 MYR, then immediately forwarded as a single 15,000 MYR outbound to an offshore company (Golden Harvest Trading). This is inconsistent with a personal account and lacks commercial rationale, indicating possible money mule activity.

matchedTypology.name: Fan-in / Fan-out (Consolidation)
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document; scam-mule guidance

verifier.note: Evidence shows a personal account with rapid consolidation of unrelated inbound payments totaling 15,500 MYR followed by an outbound transfer of 15,000 MYR to an offshore entity, matching the mule consolidation pattern rather than a legitimate merchant.

typology.name: Fan-in / Fan-out (Consolidation)
typology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document; scam-mule guidance

strDraft.activitySummary: On 14 June 2026, Marcus Lim Jia Hao received four inbound transfers totaling 15,500 MYR from four unrelated individuals within a two-hour period. Shortly after, a single outbound transfer of 15,000 MYR was made to an offshore company, Golden Harvest Trading, leaving a residual balance. This pattern of rapid consolidation and onward transmission lacks an apparent legitimate purpose for a personal account.
groundsForSuspicion: Multiple inbound transfers from four unrelated individuals in a short timeframe
Immediate consolidation and forwarding of aggregated funds to an offshore entity
No apparent relationship between senders and account holder
Transaction pattern inconsistent with a personal account and indicative of money mule activity
strDraft.recommendedAction: Escalate to the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED).

trigger: Large outbound international payments on a business account flagged by the cross-border rule

transactions.counterpartyName: Letter of Credit settlement - HSBC Trade Services
Guangzhou Textile Mills Co - Invoice INV-2291

triage.explanation: The transactions reflect a legitimate import cycle for Seri Maju Import-Export Sdn Bhd: an LC settlement from HSBC Trade Services preceded payment to Guangzhou Textile Mills and customs duty. The timing and counterparties have a clear commercial rationale, funds are partially retained, and the account is an established business. This matches the benign look-alike of high-turnover trade settlement, not pass-through laundering.

matchedTypology.name: Pass-through / Rapid Movement
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10/R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

verifier.note: The evidence shows inbound from trade settlement and outbounds to a supplier and customs, consistent with legitimate import-export activity, not irregular pass-through laundering.

trigger: Several inbound transfers from different individuals consolidated, then a payment forwarded onward; flagged by the fan-in velocity rule

triage.explanation: Personal account receives multiple inbound transfers from five unrelated individuals (Wong Siew Ling, Raj Kumar, Lim Hui Min, Tan Mei Yee, Goh Wei Liang) between 02–13 June 2026, each 850–900 MYR, totaling 4,300 MYR. Funds are aggregated then partially forwarded via a 2,000 MYR outbound to a company (Ilmu Educational Supplies Sdn Bhd). This matches the fan-in/fan-out consolidation typology—unrelated senders, no commercial rationale for a personal account, rapid forwarding, and amounts inconsistent with a personal salary or typical use. Benign look-alike (merchant/crowdfunding) is ruled out as there is no evidence of goods/services, no business account type, and the outbound is to a third-party company, not operating expenses.

matchedTypology.name: Fan-in / Fan-out (Consolidation)
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document; scam-mule guidance

verifier.note: The evidence shows partial forwarding to a company, retained balance, and consistent amounts over several days, more consistent with a legitimate merchant or crowdfunding account than mule consolidation.

typology.name: Fan-in / Fan-out (Consolidation)
typology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document; scam-mule guidance

strDraft.activitySummary: The personal account of Aisyah binti Kamal received five inward transfers from unrelated individuals—Wong Siew Ling, Raj Kumar, Lim Hui Min, Tan Mei Yee, and Goh Wei Liang—totaling MYR 4,300 between 2 and 13 June 2026. The aggregated funds were partially forwarded via a single MYR 2,000 outbound transfer to Ilmu Educational Supplies Sdn Bhd on 14 June 2026. This pattern lacks apparent economic or personal rationale and is inconsistent with the account holder's expected profile.
groundsForSuspicion: Multiple inbound credits from five unrelated individuals with no discernible relationship to the account holder
Funds aggregated over a short period (12 days) and then partially consolidated into one large outbound transfer
Onward transfer to a third-party company (Ilmu Educational Supplies Sdn Bhd) with no evident link to the account holder or personal purpose
Transaction amounts and frequency do not match the profile of a typical personal account (e.g., salary or regular expense patterns)
No underlying goods, services, or crowdfunding activity identified to explain the movement of funds
The account holder's personal running balance and activity suggest a layering or consolidation scheme rather than genuine personal transactions
strDraft.recommendedAction: Escalate to the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED).

The following day, RM6,800 was withdrawn as cash, leaving a negligible balance of RM500. Account balance returned to near zero after an additional cash withdrawal, leaving only RM500.

typology.name: Fan-in / Fan-out (Consolidation)

strDraft.activitySummary: Kevin Ng Jun Hao's personal account received five inbound transfers from five different individuals totaling MYR 24,700 within a three-hour period on 14 June 2026. Shortly after, a single outbound transfer of MYR 24,000 was made to Hai Feng Trading, an offshore entity, leaving a low balance of MYR 700. The rapid succession of unrelated credits followed by a consolidation and send-out is highly anomalous for the account's typical usage and suggests potential money mule activity.
groundsForSuspicion: Multiple (five) inbound credits from unrelated individuals within a short timeframe (approx. 3 hours).
Funds were aggregated and then forwarded onward in a single large outbound transfer to an offshore entity.
The senders (Goh Pei Shan, Ahmad Zaki bin Othman, Chong Li Wei, Siti Noraini binti Aziz, Daniel Tan Wei Sheng) have no apparent relationship to account holder Kevin Ng Jun Hao.
Transaction amounts and frequency are inconsistent with the account holder’s profile as a personal account with no known business activity.
The outbound beneficiary, Hai Feng Trading, is an offshore entity with no known nexus to the account holder, raising cross-border risk concerns.
strDraft.recommendedAction: Escalate to the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED).
